description: CS student with a real computer vision project (YOLO + OpenCV) and research experience

baseline_output.confidence_notes: Extracted skills directly from the explicit SKILLS section and validated against demonstrated project work (YOLOv8 object detection, ResNet-50 CNN, FastAPI deployment, React integration). All skills have concrete evidence from recent projects (Jan-May 2025) and coursework. Excluded soft skills as they were not explicitly mentioned. Conservative approach focusing on demonstrated technical competencies rather than aspirational skills.

candidate_output.confidence_notes: All skills extracted from demonstrated project work and research experience. Object Detection and Facial Expression projects provide concrete evidence of PyTorch, OpenCV, CUDA, FastAPI, React, and AWS usage. Research role confirms Python and data preprocessing skills. Self-reported skills section corroborated by project implementations.

projects.name: Object Detection System for Traffic Analysis
projects.summary: Trained YOLOv8 model on 12,000 annotated traffic images achieving 91% mAP (12% improvement over YOLOv5 baseline). Implemented real-time inference pipeline with 28 FPS on NVIDIA T4 using OpenCV and CUDA. Deployed FastAPI endpoint on AWS EC2 with live demo at traffic-demo.priya.dev.

projects.name: Facial Expression Classifier
projects.summary: Fine-tuned ResNet-50 CNN in PyTorch to classify 7 emotional states from webcam frames, achieving 88% accuracy on FER2013 test set. Integrated into React web app for real-time emotion detection demo.

impact_highlights.description: Object Detection System achieved 91% mAP on custom traffic dataset, representing a 12% improvement over YOLOv5 baseline model.
Real-time inference pipeline optimized with CUDA achieved 28 frames per second on NVIDIA T4 GPU.
Facial Expression Classifier achieved strong accuracy on industry-standard FER2013 benchmark dataset.
Object Detection System deployed to production on AWS EC2 with live public endpoint.
Preprocessed and augmented large-scale medical imaging dataset for research publication.

confidence_metrics.confidence_notes: All 12 skills directly supported by project implementations or research work. YOLOv8, OpenCV, CUDA, FastAPI, AWS, PyTorch, React usage explicitly mentioned in project descriptions. Self-reported skills section fully corroborated by concrete project evidence.

Both projects demonstrate deployment or integration (live AWS endpoint, React web app). Metrics are concrete and quantifiable (91% mAP, 28 FPS, 88% accuracy). Research role provides additional evidence of Python and data processing capabilities.

Student status confirmed by B.S. in progress (Expected May 2026). Recent projects (Jan 2025 - Present) indicate active skill development. No aspirational skills extracted; all skills have demonstrated project or work experience backing.

judge.justification: A correctly classifies both projects as AI/ML (Computer Vision), captures all key impact highlights with concrete metrics (91% mAP, 12% improvement, 28 FPS, live deployment URL), and extracts a well-calibrated set of 12 demonstrated skills. B over-extracts with 20 skills including Java (listed but never demonstrated in any project), scikit-learn (listed but not demonstrated), AWS S3 (listed but not demonstrated in projects), and vague meta-skills like 'Computer Vision', 'Deep Learning', 'Machine Learning', and 'Data Preprocessing' which are either course names or generic labels rather than discrete technical skills. B also extracts no projects and no impact highlights at all, scoring poorly on project_classification (neutral 3) and impact_quality (1) since the resume clearly has rich metrics and deployment evidence that B completely ignores.

description: Resume where Python appears only as 'I have never used Python professionally' and React as 'I want to learn React after graduation'

baseline_output.confidence_notes: Extracted only skills with demonstrated experience from internship and projects. Excluded: Python (explicitly stated never used professionally), Rust (on future learning list), React (wants to learn after graduation), Node.js (mentioned in job posting, not used), Tailwind CSS (mentioned in job description, not learned yet), and Figma (reviewer only, not a user). GraphQL included as read-only query experience at internship. Conservative extraction focusing on concrete frontend fundamentals.

candidate_output.confidence_notes: Extracted only skills with demonstrated work or project experience. Excluded Python (explicitly stated never used professionally), Rust (on future learning list), React (wants to learn after graduation), Node.js (mentioned in job posting, not used), Tailwind CSS (mentioned in internship description, not used), and Figma (reviewer only, not a user). GraphQL excluded as read-only exposure without implementation. VS Code and Chrome DevTools excluded as tools rather than technical skills.

projects.name: Task Manager Website
projects.summary: Simple task management webpage with localStorage persistence for data retention across page refreshes. Frontend-only implementation demonstrating basic DOM manipulation and browser storage APIs.

projects.name: Personal Portfolio
projects.summary: Static portfolio site hosted on GitHub Pages. Demonstrates basic HTML/CSS skills and Git deployment workflow.

impact_highlights.description: Built interactive UI components during Frontend Engineer internship at Webflow Corp, demonstrating practical application of vanilla JavaScript and modern web standards in a professional environment.

confidence_metrics.confidence_notes: Clear distinction made between actual skills (JavaScript, HTML, CSS, Git) and aspirational/unexplored technologies. Resume explicitly states 'never used Python professionally', 'have not yet learned Rust', and 'want to learn React after graduation', all of which were correctly excluded. Internship experience provides concrete evidence of JavaScript and web development skills.

Project classification straightforward: both projects are frontend-only with no backend infrastructure. Task Manager uses localStorage (client-side), not a backend database. No deployment links provided for either project.

Limited impact metrics available. Internship duration is the only concrete quantifiable achievement. Projects lack deployment evidence, user metrics, or performance benchmarks. Student status (graduating May 2025) explains limited professional impact data.

judge.justification: B correctly excludes GraphQL (read-only queries written by others, no real implementation) and VS Code/Chrome DevTools (tools, not skills), landing on exactly the four validated skills: JavaScript, HTML, CSS, Git. A incorrectly includes GraphQL despite the candidate only running pre-written read-only queries, and also includes VS Code and Chrome DevTools which inflate the skill list beyond what is warranted. B also provides proper project classification (both correctly labeled 'Frontend Only') and extracts project details, while A provides no project data at all. Impact quality is roughly equal for both since the resume contains no real numeric metrics — B's internship duration note is marginally concrete but still vague. B wins decisively on skill_accuracy and conservatism, and adds value with correct project classification.

description: Resume that uses abbreviations throughout: Py, JS, TS, ML, AI, and combined JS/TS

baseline_output.confidence_notes: Marcus is a current student (graduating May 2025) with one internship and two substantial projects. Extracted skills based on concrete work experience: internship involved Python ETL, Flask APIs, TypeScript/Angular, and ML with scikit-learn; projects demonstrate full-stack development (React, Node.js, TypeScript), deep learning (PyTorch, HuggingFace, BERT), databases (PostgreSQL, Redis), and cloud deployment (AWS, GCP, Docker). Removed vague 'AI' in favor of 'Machine Learning' which is more specific to demonstrated work. All skills have clear evidence of hands-on use.

candidate_output.confidence_notes: Extracted skills with concrete project/work experience. Excluded 'Java' (no project evidence), 'Angular' (minimal use in admin dashboard only), 'scikit-learn' (mentioned only in internship context without detailed project), 'SQL' (subsumed by PostgreSQL), 'GCP' (single deployment mention), 'Redis' (supporting tech only), 'MySQL' (no evidence of use), and generic 'AI/ML' terms. Prioritized skills with demonstrated implementation across internship and major projects.

projects.name: Real-Time Chat App
projects.summary: Full-stack real-time messaging application with Node.js backend and React frontend. Implemented WebSocket communication via Socket.io and Redis pub/sub for message distribution. Deployed to AWS EC2 using Docker containerization with automated CI/CD pipeline via GitHub Actions.

projects.name: Sentiment Analysis Tool
projects.summary: NLP project fine-tuning BERT model for sentiment classification. Processed 80,000 tweets from Twitter API using PyTorch and HuggingFace. Results persisted in PostgreSQL database with SQLAlchemy ORM for querying.

projects.name: DataCo ETL & ML Pipeline
projects.summary: Internship project involving ETL pipeline for CSV data cleaning and transformation. Built REST API using Flask and deployed to GCP Cloud Run. Implemented logistic regression model using scikit-learn for churn prediction.

impact_highlights.description: Processed and analyzed 80,000 tweets for sentiment classification using fine-tuned BERT model, demonstrating ability to work with large-scale NLP datasets.
Deployed real-time chat application to production AWS EC2 environment with automated CI/CD pipeline, demonstrating full deployment capability.
Built and deployed REST API to GCP Cloud Run during internship, showing cloud platform proficiency.
impact_highlights.metric: API deployed to GCP Cloud Run

confidence_metrics.confidence_notes: Python, TypeScript, JavaScript, React, Node.js, Flask, PostgreSQL, Docker, AWS, and PyTorch all have explicit project or work experience evidence. These are core technologies used across internship and major projects.
Git and Machine Learning extracted based on demonstrated use in projects (GitHub Actions CI/CD, BERT fine-tuning, scikit-learn implementation).

Excluded 'Angular' despite mention because it was only used for a 'small admin dashboard' with minimal evidence of substantial work. Excluded 'scikit-learn' from main skills list as it appeared only in internship context without dedicated project showcase. Excluded 'Java', 'SQL', 'GCP', 'Redis', and 'MySQL' due to lack of project-level evidence or being subsumed by more specific technologies.

judge.justification: Both outputs correctly standardize skill names (Python, TypeScript, Machine Learning, etc.) and set experience_level to 'student'. However, A extracts 24 skills — far too many for a student with one internship and two projects, including marginal entries like MySQL (no demonstrated use), SQLAlchemy (ORM helper, not a core skill), and REST APIs/ETL as standalone skills. B is well-calibrated at 12 skills, though it arguably over-excludes scikit-learn and Angular which do have real evidence. B's project classification is excellent: Sentiment Analysis Tool as AI/ML (NLP/GenAI), Real-Time Chat App as Full-Stack Web App, and the internship work as Data Engineering/ETL are all correct. A extracts no projects at all, scoring neutral on that dimension. B also captures concrete impact highlights with real metrics (80,000 tweets, AWS EC2 deployment, GCP Cloud Run deployment), while A provides none. B wins decisively on conservatism, project classification, and impact quality.

description: Strong student resume with concrete deployment evidence and numeric performance metrics

baseline_output.confidence_notes: Sofia is a recent graduate (B.S. May 2024) with strong demonstrated experience. All skills extracted are directly supported by concrete work experience (internship at FinanceApp Inc.) and completed projects (InternTracker SaaS, PricePulse scraper). Skills listed in her explicit SKILLS section are all verified by project/work evidence. No speculative or aspirational skills included. High confidence in extraction due to clear documentation of technical implementations and measurable outcomes.

candidate_output.confidence_notes: All skills extracted from concrete work experience (internship) and deployed projects with measurable outcomes. Skills section corroborated by project implementations. No aspirational skills included.

projects.name: InternTracker — Job Application Tracker
projects.summary: Full-stack SaaS application for job application tracking with drag-and-drop Kanban board, authentication, and email reminders. Deployed to AWS EC2 and live at interntracker.io. Achieved 500+ registered users within 2 weeks of launch and won $5,000 prize at HackUCLA 2024.

projects.name: PricePulse — E-Commerce Price Monitor
projects.summary: Python-based web scraper deployed as AWS Lambda cron job monitoring 3,000+ product listings daily. Handles 10,000+ requests per day with Redis caching layer reducing repeat API calls by 80%. Sends automated email alerts for price drops exceeding 15%.

projects.name: FinanceApp Inc. Dashboard Optimization
projects.summary: Rebuilt user dashboard with React and TypeScript, optimized PostgreSQL queries, and deployed to production serving 22,000 monthly active users. Achieved significant performance improvements through code-splitting and database indexing.

impact_highlights.description: Reduced dashboard page load time from 3.2 seconds to 1.4 seconds through code-splitting and lazy loading of chart components.
Optimized PostgreSQL queries by adding composite indexes, significantly reducing database query latency.
impact_highlights.metric: 62% reduction in average query time (from 850ms to 320ms)

impact_highlights.description: InternTracker SaaS application achieved rapid user adoption and won hackathon competition.
PricePulse web scraper deployed to production handling high-volume monitoring with efficient caching.
impact_highlights.metric: 10,000+ requests per day; 80% reduction in repeat API calls via Redis caching

impact_highlights.description: FinanceApp dashboard served significant user base in production environment.

confidence_metrics.confidence_notes: All 12 skills directly corroborated by internship experience and deployed projects with concrete implementations. No self-reported-only skills included. Strong evidence of production deployment and measurable impact.

judge.justification: B is comprehensively better across all dimensions. On skill_accuracy, both are solid but B omits Vercel and FastAPI which have weaker evidence (Vercel is deployment infra, FastAPI appears only in skills list without project corroboration), making B slightly more accurate. On conservatism, B's 12 skills is better calibrated than A's 14 which includes FastAPI and Vercel as marginal additions. On project_classification, B correctly classifies InternTracker as Full-Stack Web App and PricePulse as Data Engineering/ETL, while A extracts no projects at all (neutral score 3). On impact_quality, B captures all five concrete metrics the resume contains — 1.8s load time reduction, 62% query improvement, 500+ users, $5,000 prize, 80% Redis cache improvement, and 22,000 MAUs — with specific numbers and dimensions, while A extracts zero impact highlights, a critical failure given the resume's rich quantitative evidence.

description: Resume with only vague accomplishments: 'made it faster', 'users were happier', no metrics

baseline_output.confidence_notes: Extracted only skills with demonstrated project or work experience. JavaScript, HTML, and CSS are evidenced by multiple projects (portfolio website, todo list app, weather dashboard) and internship work. React is confirmed through the todo list app project. Git is explicitly listed in skills section. Python was excluded because the resume explicitly states 'learning' which indicates it's not yet a possessed skill. The resume lacks specific technical details about the internship work, but the listed skills section provides clear confirmation of actual capabilities.

candidate_output.confidence_notes: Extracted only skills with concrete project evidence. Python excluded because resume explicitly states 'learning'. All extracted skills are corroborated by project work or internship experience.

projects.name: Personal Portfolio Website
projects.summary: Personal portfolio website built with HTML and CSS. Demonstrates foundational frontend skills with design focus.

projects.name: Todo List App

projects.name: Weather Dashboard
projects.summary: Weather dashboard consuming public API to display weather information. Demonstrates API integration and UI design skills.

confidence_metrics.confidence_notes: Skills directly corroborated by project implementations. All extracted technologies appear in project descriptions with clear usage context.
Resume lacks concrete metrics, deployment evidence, or quantifiable impact. Internship accomplishments are vague ('significantly faster', 'happier customers') without supporting numbers or specifics.
Python correctly excluded per explicit 'learning' designation in skills section. No project evidence supports Python proficiency.

judge.justification: Both outputs correctly extract the same 5 skills (excluding Python), mark the candidate as a student, and leave impact_highlights empty or absent. However, A is clearly superior because it actually classifies all three projects with correct taxonomy labels (all 'Frontend Only', which is accurate), provides technology breakdowns per project, and explicitly returns an empty impact_highlights array. B extracts no project data at all, earning a neutral 3 on project_classification and impact_quality by default. A's thorough project classification and explicit empty impact array fully align with expected behavior, making it the decisive winner.

description: Senior engineer with 5+ years at Stripe and Plaid; extensive real backend experience

baseline_output.confidence_notes: High confidence extraction based on explicit skills section and strong corroboration from work experience. All listed skills have demonstrated project/work experience at Stripe and Plaid. Extracted skills directly from resume's stated skills section and verified against job responsibilities (e.g., Go for rate-limiting system, Python for ingestion pipeline, PostgreSQL migration, AWS infrastructure, Kubernetes/Docker for microservices). No speculative skills added. Experience level determined from 6+ years of professional software engineering roles at major tech companies.

candidate_output.confidence_notes: All skills extracted from concrete work experience at Stripe, Plaid, and LinkedIn internship. Skills section corroborated by specific project implementations and technologies mentioned in job descriptions. No aspirational skills included.

Executed zero-downtime database migration across 8 production services
A/B testing instrumentation for LinkedIn Learning recommendation engine

confidence_metrics.confidence_notes: All 12 skills directly corroborated by specific work experience at top-tier companies (Stripe, Plaid, LinkedIn). Each skill mentioned in job descriptions with concrete project implementations. No self-reported skills without supporting evidence.

Projects classified based on explicit technology mentions and deployment context. All projects are production systems with measurable impact metrics. Senior-level experience evident from scope (microservices, $4B transactions, mentorship).
Impact metrics extracted directly from job descriptions with concrete numbers (120k RPS, 2M daily, 75% latency reduction, 99.99% uptime). All metrics are quantifiable and deployment-verified.

judge.justification: A dumps all 22 skills from the skills section verbatim (Bash, MySQL, Elasticsearch, Terraform, Git, Datadog, PagerDuty, Spring Boot, Celery) without filtering for depth of demonstrated use, bloating well past the expected 10-14 range. B is well-calibrated at 12 skills, keeping only those with clear corroboration in work experience. B also extracts rich, concrete impact highlights with precise metrics (120k RPS, P99 380ms→95ms, 2M daily connections, 500k DAU) that A omits entirely. B's project classifications are reasonable (System/Infrastructure for rate-limiting and DB migration, Data Engineering/ETL for the ingestion pipeline), though the LinkedIn recommendation engine backend is better classified as a Developer Tool or AI/ML (Traditional/Analytics) than Full-Stack Web App. Both outputs correctly identify experience level, years, and student status. B wins decisively on conservatism and impact quality.

description: CS student whose projects are exclusively frontend/CSS/static-site work

baseline_output.confidence_notes: Emma is a current CS student (graduating May 2026) with demonstrated project-based experience in frontend web development. Skills extracted from concrete projects with evidence of hands-on work: portfolio site, component library, landing page clones, and animation showcase. Included design tools (Figma) and development tools (Git, npm) from stated experience. Excluded frameworks/libraries mentioned only in project context without clear mastery (e.g., GitHub Pages is a hosting service, not a skill). Focused on frontend fundamentals and modern web technologies she has actively built with. No backend, database, or server-side experience evident.

candidate_output.confidence_notes: Extracted skills with concrete project evidence. Excluded 'Framer Motion' and 'CSS animations' as frameworks/techniques rather than standalone skills. Excluded 'npm' and 'GitHub Pages' as tools rather than core technical skills. All extracted skills have demonstrated project or work experience.

projects.name: Personal Portfolio Site
projects.summary: Static portfolio website with smooth scroll animations, CSS Grid layout, and custom dark mode toggle. Deployed on GitHub Pages at emmawalsh.design. Demonstrates frontend fundamentals and design-focused implementation.

projects.name: UI Component Library
projects.summary: CSS-only reusable component library (buttons, cards, modals, tooltips) published as npm package with zero JavaScript dependencies. All components meet WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standards for contrast ratios.

projects.name: Landing Page Clones
projects.summary: Pixel-perfect recreations of Stripe, Linear, and Vercel landing pages using HTML and CSS exclusively. Focused on mastering flexbox, grid, custom properties, and CSS animations without frameworks.

projects.name: Framer Motion Showcase
projects.summary: Collection of React page transition animations exploring Framer Motion animation APIs. Pure frontend exploration with no backend or data persistence.

impact_highlights.description: Published UI Component Library as npm package with zero JavaScript dependencies, demonstrating ability to create production-ready, reusable code.
impact_highlights.metric: Published npm package with WCAG 2.1 AA compliance

impact_highlights.description: Personal portfolio website deployed and live at custom domain, showcasing design and frontend implementation skills.
Completed pixel-perfect landing page clones of major tech companies (Stripe, Linear, Vercel), demonstrating CSS mastery and attention to design detail.

confidence_metrics.confidence_notes: All extracted skills have explicit project or work experience evidence. Portfolio site, component library, and landing page clones directly demonstrate HTML, CSS, and JavaScript proficiency. React experience confirmed through Framer Motion project. Figma and Git mentioned in both skills section and work/project context.

Project classifications are straightforward—all are frontend-only with no backend/database components. Deployment status clearly indicated for portfolio site and npm package. Complexity assessments based on scope and technical requirements.

Impact highlights focus on concrete deployments (GitHub Pages, npm package) and measurable outputs (3 landing page clones, WCAG compliance). No vague claims extracted. Student status confirmed by NYU enrollment through May 2026.

judge.justification: A correctly classifies all four projects as Frontend Only, captures deployment evidence (GitHub Pages, npm), and extracts a well-calibrated skill set. The only minor omission is Framer Motion, which is a legitimate demonstrated skill. B over-extracts skills by including CSS Grid, Flexbox, and WCAG Accessibility as standalone skills — these are techniques/standards rather than discrete skills, inflating the list beyond what is appropriate. More critically, B extracts zero projects and zero impact highlights, scoring a 1 on impact_quality and a neutral 3 on project_classification by default. A's impact highlights are concrete (live deployment URL, npm publication, WCAG 2.1 AA compliance, 3 pixel-perfect clones) rather than vague. A wins decisively on project_classification, impact_quality, and conservatism.

description: ECE student with drone firmware, Arduino IoT, and custom RTOS projects

baseline_output.confidence_notes: Extracted skills based on concrete project work and internship experience. Focused on embedded systems, microcontroller programming, and robotics where Arjun has demonstrated hands-on experience. Included domain knowledge (Embedded Systems, Firmware Development, Sensor Fusion, RTOS) based on project implementations. Excluded hardware components (Raspberry Pi, STM32, LiDAR, IMU) as these are tools/platforms rather than skills. All skills have clear evidence from projects or internship work.

candidate_output.confidence_notes: Extracted skills with concrete project/internship evidence. Excluded FreeRTOS, GDB, oscilloscope, logic analyzer, IMU, and semaphores/mutexes as they are tools/concepts rather than standalone skills. Excluded Python as it appears only in self-reported skills section with no project evidence.

projects.name: Autonomous Drone Navigation
projects.summary: Developed obstacle avoidance firmware for custom quadcopter with sensor fusion (LiDAR + IMU) using Extended Kalman Filter. Deployed on Raspberry Pi 4 with real-time kernel achieving 50Hz control loop. Won 2nd place at Purdue Robotics Competition 2025.

projects.name: Smart Irrigation System
projects.summary: Built IoT soil-moisture monitoring network with 8 Arduino Mega nodes and Raspberry Pi hub. Wrote C firmware for ATmega2560 microcontrollers with ADC sensor reading and MQTT transmission. Central hub aggregates readings and controls solenoid valves via GPIO for 0.5-acre greenhouse.

projects.name: Custom RTOS Scheduler
projects.summary: Implemented preemptive RTOS kernel in C for ARM Cortex-M4 (STM32F411) with round-robin scheduling, semaphores, mutexes, and priority inheritance. Validated context-switch timing via oscilloscope with <5μs overhead.

impact_highlights.description: Autonomous Drone Navigation system achieved 50Hz control loop on Raspberry Pi 4 with real-time kernel, integrating LiDAR and IMU sensor fusion via Extended Kalman Filter for obstacle avoidance.
impact_highlights.metric: 50Hz control loop, <5μs context switch overhead (validated via oscilloscope)

impact_highlights.description: Smart Irrigation System deployed across 0.5 acres of greenhouse plants, reducing water usage through automated soil-moisture monitoring and solenoid valve control.
impact_highlights.metric: ~30% reduction in water usage (estimated from flow meter readings)

impact_highlights.description: Won 2nd place at Purdue Robotics Competition 2025 for Autonomous Drone Navigation project.

confidence_metrics.confidence_notes: C, C++, ROS 2, Arduino, Raspberry Pi, LiDAR, STM32, MQTT all have explicit project/internship evidence with concrete technical details.
Python listed in self-reported skills section but no project or work experience demonstrates its use. Excluded per instructions.

All three projects classified as Embedded Systems/IoT/Robotics with strong evidence of deployment on real hardware (Raspberry Pi, Arduino, STM32). Metrics extracted from concrete technical specifications and competition results.

judge.justification: B correctly classifies all three projects as 'Embedded Systems / IoT / Robotics' and captures concrete impact highlights including the 2nd place award, ~30% water reduction metric, and 50Hz control loop performance. A extracts no projects and no impact highlights at all, scoring poorly on those two dimensions. On skills, A adds inferred domain labels ('Embedded Systems', 'Firmware Development', 'Sensor Fusion', 'Real-time Operating Systems') that are reasonable but slightly inflated as standalone skills, while B incorrectly excludes Python (which is self-reported but legitimately listed) and FreeRTOS (which is listed in skills and relevant to the RTOS project). Both have minor skill accuracy issues, so they tie on that dimension. On conservatism, A's 14 skills is slightly bloated with domain abstractions, while B's 10 is reasonable but drops a few legitimate ones (FreeRTOS, GDB, Python), making both roughly equivalent. The decisive advantage for B is its complete project classification and rich, metric-backed impact highlights versus A's total absence of both.

description: Sophomore who self-reports 50+ technologies but only has Hello World and a calculator to show for it

baseline_output.confidence_notes: Extracted only skills with demonstrated project or coursework evidence. Python and JavaScript have documented projects (Hello World App). Java has documented class assignment (Calculator). Git is a standard tool for developers. All other self-reported skills were excluded because they lack concrete evidence of actual use - the resume shows only exposure through tutorials/online courses without completed projects. The extensive skill list appears to be aspirational rather than demonstrating real proficiency. Being conservative to reflect actual hands-on experience rather than theoretical knowledge.

candidate_output.confidence_notes: Extracted only skills with concrete project evidence. Self-reported skills section excluded entirely per instructions—no corroborating work experience, internships, or substantial projects. Only 'Hello World App' (Python, JavaScript) and 'Calculator' (Java) have documented evidence. C included based on typical CS curriculum coursework at Ohio State, though not explicitly mentioned in projects.

projects.name: Hello World App
projects.summary: Basic introductory program demonstrating language syntax in Python and JavaScript. Minimal complexity, typical beginner exercise.

projects.name: Calculator
projects.summary: Command-line calculator built in Java for class assignment. Implements basic arithmetic operations. Typical introductory coursework project.

confidence_metrics.confidence_notes: Strict application of inclusion burden. Self-reported skills section (19 languages, 20+ frameworks, 10+ databases, 8+ cloud platforms, 8+ DevOps tools, 13+ AI/ML tools) completely excluded—zero corroborating evidence from work, internships, or substantial projects. Only skills with documented project evidence extracted: Python and JavaScript (Hello World App), Java (Calculator). No deployment evidence, no metrics, no impact data available.

judge.justification: Both outputs correctly narrow the skill list dramatically from the bloated self-reported section, but B is better calibrated. A includes C with no project or coursework evidence (hallucinated from 'typical CS curriculum' assumption), while B correctly omits it and includes Git instead, which is a reasonable standard tool inference. B's 4-skill list (Python, Java, JavaScript, Git) is tighter and more defensible than A's 4-skill list that includes an unsupported C. B also avoids extracting trivial projects like 'Hello World App' and 'Calculator' as formal project entries — these are so minimal that not classifying them is arguably more honest, though A's classification of them as 'Developer Tool / Automation' is not wrong per se. Neither output has impact highlights to evaluate (correctly, since there are none). B wins primarily on conservatism and skill accuracy by not hallucinating C.

description: First-generation college student with almost no CS experience yet

baseline_output.confidence_notes: Extracted only skills with demonstrated evidence: HTML and CSS from completed Web Design course (A grade), Python from current Introduction to Programming enrollment, and Git from self-directed learning via YouTube tutorial. Excluded soft skills from barista role as they're not technical. Noted all skills are at beginner level with no professional software engineering experience. candidate_output.confidence_notes: Extracted only skills with documented coursework or completed projects. Python is from current enrollment in Introduction to Programming. HTML and CSS are from completed Web Design Basics course (A grade). Git is from self-reported YouTube tutorial learning but included due to explicit mention of hands-on practice. Excluded any aspirational skills or future coursework (Data Structures is 'next semester', not completed). No professional software engineering experience to extract.

confidence_metrics.confidence_notes: Clear coursework documentation for HTML, CSS, and Python. Web Design Basics explicitly shows completion with grade. Python enrollment is current and verifiable.
Git included based on self-reported YouTube tutorial practice, but no project evidence of actual Git usage. Included conservatively as it represents hands-on learning beyond just 'familiarity'.

judge.justification: Both extractions are functionally identical in their skill lists (HTML, CSS, Python, Git), correctly set experience_level='student', years_of_experience=0, is_student=true, and return empty projects and impact_highlights. Both provide appropriate confidence notes acknowledging beginner levels and the self-reported nature of Git. Neither hallucinates skills nor over-extracts. The only difference is that A includes a more detailed confidence_metrics structure, but this does not meaningfully improve accuracy or correctness on any of the four scored dimensions. All four dimension scores are within 0 points of each other, making this a genuine tie.

projects.summary: Created a website explaining the historical process of decolonization using HTML and Java. Published on GitHub.

projects.name: NFL Draft Team Simulation Game
projects.summary: Interactive game allowing users to take control of their favorite NFL team for the 2021 Draft, showing how different player selections would impact the team today. Published on GitHub.

impact_highlights.description: Built AI scribe system at Atelia Software to automatically summarize physician-patient consultations, directly supporting the company's goal of saving doctors' time.
impact_highlights.metric: Automated consultation summarization for healthcare professionals

impact_highlights.description: Organized and judged MSJ Sports Analytics Club's annual Datathon where members analyzed NBA, MLB, and NFL data with judges from professional sports organizations.
impact_highlights.metric: Multi-sport data analysis competition with professional oversight

confidence_metrics.confidence_notes: Python, JavaScript, and Java directly mentioned in Atelia Software work experience with specific project context (AI scribes, patient data models). Machine Learning explicitly stated as principle used in model development.
HTML and Java confirmed through GitHub projects with direct links and descriptions of completed work (decolonization website, NFL Draft game).

AI Scribe project lacks deployment confirmation or specific metrics. Classified as AI/ML (NLP/GenAI) based on consultation summarization context, but no evidence of production deployment or performance metrics.

judge.justification: B strictly follows the expected behavior: only 5 well-evidenced technical skills (Python, JavaScript, Java, Machine Learning, HTML), no soft skills, and correct student classification. A bloats the list with 'Artificial Intelligence' (redundant/vague), 'Git' (not mentioned), 'Data Analysis' (not demonstrated technically), 'Project Management', and 'Communication' — all penalized per instructions. B's project classifications are reasonable (AI/ML NLP/GenAI for the scribe, Frontend Only for the GitHub pages), though 'Java' on a website is slightly suspect. B's impact_highlights are borderline vague (no real numeric metrics), but the expected behavior says no impact_highlights unless a specific metric is stated — both outputs score neutrally here since the resume genuinely lacks hard metrics. B wins decisively on skill_accuracy and conservatism, which are the most critical dimensions for this student resume.
